speaker: interviewer
text: And camera's at speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that Yaffa stayed in Cluj. Cluj. And at that time, it was Kolozsvar. Kolozsvar. Because she knew her fiance had survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: She heard, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then she was going to wait there for him. And you and Miriam then went where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- each time, we were told which city to go in, because they had displaced persons camps. They called them DP camps for us. And we from Cluj-- from Kolozsvar we went to Budapest, because that was the main headquarters for gathering the survivors, the orphans of the war. And then from there, we were sent to Szombathely, which was another camp, displaced persons camp, where the survivors stayed until they could find transport to go to Palestine. And then from Szombathely we went to Graz. Each time days passed by before we were told, OK, now you can move on. And they would take us by buses and transportation to the next city. Then was Graz, which was in Austria. And then from Graz we went to Linz, which was another displaced person-- it   was a big displaced persons camp in Linz, where some people stayed for a long time. And a lot of survivors have been to this camp. But we stayed for a short time there. And then from Linz we went to Salzburg, which is also in Austria. From Salzburg to Brivio, which was in Italy, and that was the last stop before Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But let's talk a little bit about Salzburg, because you stayed there the longest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of any of the DP camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we stayed in Salzburg for five months.

speaker: interviewer
text: For five months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in these DP camps, did you found the people kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the DP camp, usually it meant that we took over-- they took over like a hospital that formally was maybe an Austrian hospital, or a motel-- not a motel, we didn't have motels in those days-- a hotel, or a large house. And they would set it up for the survivors. I mean for the, for the displaced persons. And so it wasn't anything special. I mean, it was just putting in beds and giving us blankets.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were warm, and then we had--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were fed, and we had food. And the overseas organizations and the Jewish Agency-- everybody, now they had sent helpers over to organize the setup and to try and get food from Palestine. The Jewish Brigade people were sent over. And then I joined. And all the organizations from the world were sending food and clothing. And so we had-- we were pretty comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by the time you got to Salzburg, you and Miriam were part of a group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And as we traveled from somewhere to Graz, Linz, and then Salzburg, we met other young people who survived. And they were Hungarians, Romanians, and from Poland. And they were interested. We were all in the Zionist Youth. We were interested in forming a kibbutz in Palestine. So we joined as a group. And then we tried to travel from place to place as a group. And by the time we reached Salzburg, we were a group of 42 boys and girls, all between the ages of 15 and 23. And we kind of set up headquarters in the barracks. They were like barracks, too, that we lived in. And we set up headquarters like our future kibbutz. We were going to, you know, share chores, and work, learn how to do different things, and distribute the responsibilities, and learn Hebrew, and sing songs, and try and get on a ship to transfer us to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know that Salzburg is where you met your husband Harold. And can you tell us how that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's-- I always like to tell people it's like the so many similar movies that I've seen that-- we were in this barrack. And we were, you know, two layers of bunks. And Harold was stationed in the American Army in Salzburg. And he asked around where he could meet Jewish boys and girls, because he wanted to practice his Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody directed him to our camp. And so he came to visit us. And the very first day-- he's with his American uniform-- the very first day he came to visit us, we were, you know, very-- everybody-- he was so special. I mean, he was an American soldier. And so we were dancing, and we were singing. And when-- the famous dance and song where the young people used to do in Europe is where it talks about how the train is traveling through the mountains, and the rocks. And then, of all the girls I pick you to dance with. And then the girl goes-- in the same song-- and then says, of all the boys I pick you to dance. Can you sing it for us, Penina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I can still sing it because I heard it so many times here, too. It goes-- my beautiful voice, but it goes like-- [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] And then again, [NON-ENGLISH] means 'the boy' in [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, 'I love you. I want to pick you.' It's not 'pick you,' but, 'I love you.' So when they danced, and then the girl picked Harold. And then when Harold's turn to pick-- came to pick a girl, he motioned, he went over. And he put his hand out, and he asked me to come. So at first, I didn't want to come dance with him. Then finally, I did come. After the third try, I did come to dance with him. And when I came down to dance with him, they all started to applaud and say, ah-ha, ah-ha, hey, you la la, you like. They're meaning, OK, this is the boy that met the girl, that really likes the girl. So we danced. So that was the very first time when we met.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we couldn't talk to each other, because I didn't-- I couldn't speak English. Yeah, I didn't know Hebrew. We couldn't communicate. So we just, we had an interpreter. And Harold said to this friend of mine, [NON-ENGLISH], that I'll come back tomorrow to visit. And tell Peszi I want to see her again. And so we started communicating like that. And he came back the next day. And he came back as much as he could from the army, you know, on his furlough and visited us. And then he told me he wants to teach me Hebrew. And so I said, fine, I want to learn Hebrew, because we're going to Palestine, and I want to know Hebrew. And so we talked like happy talk with an interpreter. And as soon as I started learning Hebrew, the first word-- I mean one of the important words was the opposite, the word opposite. And I felt that once I knew that word, I know so much. Because I didn't know what hot was, but I could say cold. So I would say the opposite of hot, the opposite of small, the opposite of sweet. So whenever I knew one word, I would say 'the opposite,' and he would give me the word. And I still have my notebook where I wrote down all these words that I was studying. And then the best way he was teaching me, the songs, and to learn the words to the songs. And you know Hebrew. And we were dating with an interpreter, so we were three. And three is a crowd, so I was determined to learn Hebrew fast. And after about three months, I think, I was speaking Hebrew with him. And, and we were able to-- we went to concerts. We saw in the city of Salzburg. And Harold brought me gifts all the time, just little gifts, like handkerchiefs from France and flowers. And we made a date. And by the time we-- Harold was going to be shipped back to the United States to be discharged from the army. And this was March. And by that time, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: March of '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: March of '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war ended. '46, yes. So we made a date across the ocean. Harold said he's going to come. Everybody started liking him. And he wanted to stay and become a member of our kibbutz in Palestine. So he made a promise that he was going to come and meet me across the ocean. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Palestine, yes. And so by the time he left-- and he couldn't give me anything like an engagement ring because he didn't have any. So he gave me this silver Mogen David necklace, which I had until recently, when it was stolen. But that was our official engagement ring that he gave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me his address, where to write in the army. And then he was going back to Chicago to be discharged from the army and then come to meet me in Palestine. So you were in Salzburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Salzburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in March of--

speaker: interviewer
text: --'46. And then how long-- how much longer were you in Salzburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Salzburg until August--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Until the beginning of August. Then we had to wait for ships to be able to get to Palestine. And the Jewish Agency, they were chartering ships from the Italian fishermen to take transports of the survivors to Palestine, like the Exodus story.

speaker: interviewer
text: The illegal ships.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, illegal ships, right. They were fishing boats that they were pretending to bring past food-- I mean fishing stuff. Instead, there were passengers. And we went-- from Salzburg we went to Italy. And we had to wait in Brivio. We had to wait until we got a ship there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, what was the name of the ship? And when did you get on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now finally, we-- the name of the ship was And finally in August, we-- we were able to sail on this ship to Palestine. But unfortunately, by the time-- we were staying in Europe an awful long time in the displaced persons camp, because we were helping out in this camp in Salzburg. And by the time we were able to go to Palestine, the British decided they would not let any more immigrants in. So they decided that, and by the time we went, they captured, they captured our ship. And we were sent to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you one thing. Had you still not heard anything about your brother at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still did not know anything about your brother. I'm sorry, go ahead. So they stopped the ship, and they put you off in Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cyprus. And we were on the water for three days drifting and then finally ended up in Cyprus. And then in Cyprus--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this three days of drifting. You had problems with the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, our ship broke down. The motor broke down. And then they were-- they, they couldn't continue with the motor. So we were just drifting on the seas. And the conditions were terrible because we were running out of food and water. And we were not-- we weren't able to go up to be spotted by the British. They were afraid the British would spot you and then deport you to Cyprus. So we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Up on deck?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't go on deck. We took turns.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were down under--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were covered with canvas. And we took turns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 800 on this ship, which was built for fishing, maybe for 80. We were five tiers of bunks, just packed like sardines.

speaker: interviewer
text: And drifting?

speaker: interviewee
text: And drifting, right. And drifting three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that create problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then finally they-- it was, it was very-- the conditions were very hard. Some people wished they had died in the camps because it was so bad. Because they were hungry and they were throwing up and sick and all that. And they just wished they had died because it was that bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they put you off in Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cyprus. And I, my sister and I, I had the encouragement of reading the letters from Harold that we were going to meet in Palestine. So I was reading my love letters. And that's what kept me from becoming sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ship, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you got to Cyprus, tell me about the DP camp on Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Cyprus DP camp was-- the conditions were terrible. They were, you know, like under tents, just boards that we slept on, got blanket and-- and we got very little food, very little more than what we got in Auschwitz, except we didn't have barbed wires and we didn't-- we had barbed wires, but we didn't have a crematorium and gas chambers. But the conditions were terrible. We didn't have any utensils. From cans, we made makeshift ovens. And we just slept on boards with blankets. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were guarded?

speaker: interviewee
text: The place was enclosed with barbed wires. And the British would walk through every now and then. It was just enclosed. And we would get newspaper people and visitors every now and then from outside. But we were, we were there for several months, about three months. And we just like made, made the best of it. We tried to be happy because we were out of Europe, out of Auschwitz, out of that. But Cyprus, we didn't know what the future was going to bring. And we were, we were pretty worried.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any representatives from any United States agencies, or government agencies, or UNRRA, or any of the national aid--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they had official people come all the time to Cyprus from the United States government, from I guess, the joint, from Isriael-- from Palestine, from the Jewish Agency. There were officials coming all the time. And especially, there were a lot of British newspaper people coming. And they were taking pictures for the newspapers. And we were photographed all the time. And after I got out of there, I went to this photo place. And I succeeded in, in getting a few of the photographs that were taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which we're going to see at the end of the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were taken. But we were-- in Cyprus, we didn't know where the other people were. We didn't know where the other survivors who were on different ships-- we had no idea what happened to them. And we-- I was in Cyprus and trying to establish contact with Harold again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, tell us about that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was very hard. We-- we-- we were in-- went from Salzburg, from when I said goodbye to him, I wrote to him, and I forgot to put my address on. Innocent for 18-year-old who thinks that just by putting down my name, Peszi Weisz, and the country, that my letters were going to arrive. So he couldn't write to me. And it took a long time for us to establish contact. Finally, I was going to give up because I thought it was one of those romances that, OK, you know, it was nice. And it ended. And he's not coming to meet me. He's not writing to me. But my fellow kibbutzniks, all the people in my group, said, no. I must try to get in touch with Harold because they all loved him, and they knew he's, he's going to write.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we put together 15 of us-- 15 of the people in the kibbutz put together pennies that we earned by going out and doing chores, and scrubbing, and stuff like that. And they put together their pennies in order to send a telegram. And when I sent the telegram to Harold in Chicago-- you had to put down your return address. They wouldn't send a telegram with-- so that's when he finally got my address. And I got a telegram back saying that, OK, I got your letter, so now I have your address. I will write. So that's how we established, but-- and in Cyprus, the British correspondents were coming in. And I smuggled-- no, I didn't have to really smuggle-- I gave him a letter telling him that I am trying to meet my fiance from America. And he  was supposed to be there already. And he delivered the letter to Harold. And we finally-- In Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Palestine-- that I was already in Cyprus. And before then, we were corresponding in Europe, when I was first corresponding in Cyp-- and then Brivio-- not in Cyp-- in Salzburg, but in Brivio. I have a whole stack of letters. I still have them all that he wrote to me and I wrote back to him. Our romance was 18 months before we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you next see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw him next when we were-- when I arrived in Palestine. The British--

speaker: interviewer
text: But not on Cyprus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on Cyprus, no. In Cyprus, they gave us three certificates. And our group of 42 were allowed to let three people leave. And so they let me leave because they knew Harold was waiting. And when I got to-- I got to Palestine with this one couple. And we were in camp. I was put in camp under the British guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Palestine now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Palestine, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Athlit, which was a gathering point for all the incoming illegal immigrants. It was Athlit. And then another camp-- I think it was called Qiryat Shemu'el, something like that. That was specially set up-- after they processed your papers and made sure that you were an immigrant with the immigration papers, and you weren't sick, and you didn't bring in any diseases. So we were put in this camp for a whole month. And the first time Harold came to visit me in this camp under the British guard, and I-- was barbed wires. He couldn't come in just from the outside. And I stuck my hand out through the barbed wire to shake his hand. And I didn't even know I was scratched or that I was bleeding. But that's how we-- the first time we met. And then the British Guard said 10 minutes were up. And then we had to leave. But the way we established contact was, when I got into Palestine, I wrote a little note on a piece of paper and threw it out the window to a little girl, about 12 year old. And I wrote on it to please bring it to the Technion, where Harold was studying under the GI Bill, because the British wouldn't let people in. So he went on the pretense of being a student. So he was studying. And this little piece of paper said in Hebrew, [HEBREW]. 'I have arrived.' And this little girl made sure that Harold got it at the Technion. And that's how he found out that I have arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- so you and Harold were--

speaker: interviewee
text: We just met there.

speaker: interviewer
text: --reuinited.

speaker: interviewee
text: Reunited. And we had to wait until I was liberated completely out of camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was-- we went to this-- I went to this makeshift kibbutz, which was in Binyamina, a town about 30 miles from Haifa. And Harold was starting at the Technion and lived in the separate headquarters. And then he came, and he joined our group as an official member. And the group let him continue studying at the Technion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was this your whole group that had gotten out of Cyprus?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I stayed with a cousin. And my group didn't get-- I got out in August, September. And my group didn't get out until December. So I waited.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Miriam was with the group, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miriam was with the group. So Harold and I waited until March of '47, until the whole group got out and my sister got out. And we were married in March 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the whole group there?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the whole group. And then my pictures are in this Binyamina, which was a small town at the time. It's still a small town. And all the children of the village came to watch, and the kibbutz people. And you'll see some children in the background. But that was-- that's where we were married, in Binyamina.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how long did you stay in Palestine before you came back to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there almost a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Almost a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: A little less than a year. Harold wanted to continue his studies. And conditions in Palestine were terrible at that time because the British were still there, and the Jewish people were trying to get them out. And bombs were going off all over. And the curfews-- you couldn't leave, and you had to be back by a certain hour in the evening. And it just was-- this was October of 1947. The United Nations voted on the partition in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we were there-- we were married in March. And then April, May, June-- the entire time, conditions were very, very bad at that time in Israel. And we decided, kind of spur-of-the-moment, that Harold will go back to Chicago and finish his education and then come back to Palestine. So after we came back, it was just a month later that the partition was voted upon. And then in April, Israel was established. So you left to come back to America when?

speaker: interviewee
text: To America. In October of 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: October of '47, you came back. Now, and what about your sister, Miriam?

speaker: interviewee
text: She stayed with the kibbutz. She stayed with the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: She stayed with the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she stayed with the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and Harold came to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we  went--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me about your first impressions of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my first impression, I thought it was overwhelming, and beautiful, and big. And when we arrived in New York, I was-- we were walking across the street, and I started counting how many floors the Empire State Building had. And I just made sure I went to see the Statue of Liberty. Because we children, we read a lot about history. We, we, we, you know, knew about the Statue of Liberty and what it said on it and all that. And I just-- it was very exciting and also very, very hard. Because when I came here, I could not speak not one single English word. And so I had-- we were raised with three languages, like Yiddish, and Hungarian, and Romanian. And we studied Hebrew in school. And then I learned French, a little French. And then I learned German. So when I came here, I knew six languages, and I had to learn another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was fighting it for a while. And I realized I would just have to-- Harold and I communicated in Hebrew. So I said, why do I have to learn another language? But you live in America, you learn American. You learn English. And I studied very hard. I mean, I took correspondence courses and Americanization classes and loved it, because I was studying all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just thought the food, and the clothing, and-- well, it took me a long time to adjust. Like I wanted to wear bobby socks all the time. We were going to this elegant party at the Palmer House in Chicago. And my mother-in-law-- my sister-in-law's best friend was getting married at the Palmer House in Chicago. And my mother-in-law said, you're not going with bobby socks. And I, and I said, well, this-- bobby socks, to me, were something very special. Because as children, we couldn't wear bobby socks because it was not-- we were too religious. It was too modern. So when I came here, everything-- bobby socks, I would buy one in every color. And I was so proud I could wear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- we had this big argument. I didn't want to wear lipstick. I had to learn every imaginable thing. But I was determined to make this my happy place in life. And I took correspondence courses and learned how to type, and psychology course, and sewing-- beginners, intermediate, advanced-- and tailoring. I took all these courses.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my mother-in-law taught me Mahjong and Bridge and Kaluki. And, and I joined the bowling league. And I became pretty Americanized. I mean, I just took one class after another.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question. When did you ever find out that your brother Mordechia was alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were in, in Italy, a group of people were passing through. And they said, did you know that your Mordechai, your brother, survived? That's when I found out, when we were in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, he was liberated from Dachau. And he was sent, you know, to hospital. And when he was well enough, he also went back to Cluj, to Koloszvar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he also signed up to go on [NON-ENGLISH], to go back to Palestine. And his ship also was caught and broke down. And, matter of fact, his ship had hit a, hit a rock. And they lost about 13 people from the ship there. It sank, and they were rescued. And eventually, he ended up in Palestine. And is still in Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never-- I didn't him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you had already left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. No, no, I take it back. In March-- we left in October. I think in August he came to visit us. About a month or two before we left, he came to see us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family, you were reunited, the two sisters, you and  Miriam.

speaker: interviewee
text: My two sisters, my Miriam, and I, and Mordechai, we were able to see each other for a month or two. And we took some pictures, yes. And he's, he's still in Israel. And my older sister Yaffa is in Israel. She-- and from Romania, the conditions in Romania were getting bad. So she, she stayed until 1959. And then she got out. And now she's in Israel. And my sister is in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit to, to you are in to be Americanized. How did you find your experiences in the Holocaust affected you in building your life and so forth as an American?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found that a lot of people couldn't speak about it. And they, they kind of, you know, had just wanted to put it aside and not think about it. And I thought it was very important, because I saw some of my fellow survivors who were wallowing in pity. And they couldn't-- physically, they survived. But mentally, they, you know, they didn't. And they were destroying themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided I'm going to live a life and be in equilibrium. I came across this word in my dictionary. And I decided mental balance, that's the key to everything. So I'm going to remember my past, and make sure that it's not going to happen again, and try speaking about it, and teach the Holocaust lesson. But at the same time, be happy and live every day to the fullest and just not to let that stop me from being happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I found that just by going out to speaking to groups-- and I've spoken to schools and organizations and garden clubs and art, and B'nai B'rith, and Hadas. You name it, all the organizations. I was doing it in Chicago, and then when we lived in Florida, and also in Houston, and a little bit now. And I found that, that it's so important, that even if it's hard for me to do it, and it refreshes memories, and it brings back thoughts that I don't want to think about every day-- you cannot think about the Holocaust every day of your life. If you do, you do not exist. You destroy yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have to just kind of put it aside and say, OK, now this happened. I want to prevent it from happening, educate people, get them involved, stop the little things that-- the wrong thing that happens at the beginning. But don't live with it every day. Good.
